title: Why Jaxon Smith-Njigba is the most efficient WR in the NFL
text:
High-volume receivers in the NFL aren’t usually the most efficient when it comes to it. Throw a guy the ball 150 times he is obviously going to put up some numbers. It’s about what they do with it once they catch the ball. Players like Justin Jefferson, DK Metcalf, and Courtland Sutton struggled this season to be efficient at high volume.
That was the exact opposite story for Jaxon Smith-Njigba. He wasn’t the highest volume receiver in the NFL, but nobody came close to what he could do with once he got the ball. JSN also wasn’t just the NFL receiving yards leader but the most efficient wideout this past season of the National Football League.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggalt)

Reclogs <- read_csv("2025NFLRec.csv")

receivers <- Reclogs|>
  filter(Pos == "WR", Tgt >= 100) |>
  group_by(Player) |>
  mutate(
    catch_rate = Rec / Tgt
  ) |> ungroup()

top20 <- receivers |> 
  top_n(15, wt=catch_rate) |>
  arrange(desc(catch_rate))

JSN <- top20 |>
  filter(
    Player == "Jaxon Smith-Njigba"
  )
ggplot() +
  geom_bar(
    data=top20, 
    aes(x=reorder(Player, catch_rate), weight=catch_rate), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(
    data=JSN, 
    aes(x=reorder(Player, catch_rate), weight=catch_rate),
    fill="#69BE28"
    ) +
    labs(
     title = "Jaxon Smith-Njigba is among the leagues best in hauling in catches",
     subtitle = "The Seahawks star receiver is top 3 in the NFL in catch rate behind two all-pro wideouts.",
     x = "",
     y = "catch rate",
     caption = "Source: Pro Football Reference | By Ben Ofe"
     ) +
  coord_flip() + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Efficiency for a receiver isn’t just about the total yardage that they get. Jaxon Smith-Njigba has developed into a very reliable target for Seahawks quarterback Sam Darnold, thanks to his elite separation and ability to make difficult catches look easy. One of the key statistics that highlights this is his catch rate percentage amongst other top receivers in the NFL. Smith-Njigba, who is top 3 in the NFL in catch rate behind two all-wideouts, makes him a key part in keeping drives and plays alive.


Code
middle <- Reclogs |>
filter(Pos == "WR", Tgt >= 50) 

toprec <- Reclogs |>
  filter(Pos == "WR", Tgt >= 125, Yds >= 1200)

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=Reclogs, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=middle, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds), color="black")+
  geom_point(data=toprec, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds), color="red") +
  geom_point(data = JSN, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds), color="#69BE28") + 
  geom_text_repel(data=toprec, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds, label=Player))+
  geom_encircle(data=toprec, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds), s_shape=.5, expand=.03, colour="red") +
  geom_text(aes(x=115, y=1300, label="Top Receivers")) + 
  labs(title="Smith-Njigba does more with less compared to the rest", 
       subtitle="Compared to other wide receivers in the NFL the Seattle Seahawks receiver outputs more yards on less catches.",
       x="Targets",
       y="Receiving Yards",
       caption = "Source: Pro Football Reference | By Ben Ofe") +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









As you can see in the chart, Jamar Chase, Amon Ra St. Brown, and Puka Nacua led the league in volume, but Jaxon Smith-Njigba maximized every opportunity that came his way. He, in turn, added more production this year on less receptions than any other top receiver in the league at the position, this year showing again how efficient he is. That is what efficiency is all about doing more with less.


Code
recvtargets <- Reclogs|>
  filter(Pos == "WR", Tgt >= 150) |>
  group_by(Player) 


ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=recvtargets, 
    aes(
      y=Player, yend=Player,
      x= `Y/R`, xend= `Y/Tgt`)
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=recvtargets,
    aes(
      y=Player,
      x=`Y/R`,
      color="Yards per Reception"
    ), size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=recvtargets,
    aes(
      y=Player,
      x=`Y/Tgt`,
      color="Yards per Target"
    ), size=2
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(name="Receiving", values=c("#002244","#69BE28")) +
labs(title="A look at how the top NFL WRs turn Receptions into Yards", 
       subtitle="Jaxon Smith-Njigba leads the top receivers in the NFL with yards after catch.",
       x="Yards per Target vs Yards per Reception",
       y="",
       caption = "Source: Pro Football Reference | By Ben Ofe") +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Now this is where JSN excels and does his dirty work here. The green dot is yards per target that shows the yardage of a ball thrown the way caught or not, and the navy dot is what he averages when he makes a catch. You can see on this dumbbell chart that Smith-Njigba leads in the NFL with his 15.1 yards per reception in this stat by a WIDE margin. Before he even catches the ball, you can see that he almost already has all-pro guys like Jamar Chase and Chris Olave beat with what they do on each reception. This is largely in part due to his ability to take the short and medium yardage catches and get upfield to turn them into something more.
The common denominator that comes up here in every chart is that JSN accomplishes more with less than other receivers in the NFL. He catches everything that Sam Darnold throws his way, plays with a ton of efficiency, and makes things happen after the catch. That’s exactly what makes him the most effective wide receiver.
